label: Bogotá
sub: Capital and seat of government; founded 1538 by Jiménez de Quesada on Muisca Bacatá. Site of Bogotazo (1948) and Palace of Justice siege (1985).

label: Medellín
sub: Home of Pablo Escobar's Medellín Cartel. Homicide capital of the world in 1991 (380/100k); remarkable transformation by 2010s through urban innovation.

label: Cartagena de Indias
sub: Founded 1533 by Pedro de Heredia. Principal Spanish colonial port, slave trade entry point, and site of 2016 FARC peace accord signing ceremony.

label: Cali
sub: Former stronghold of the Cali Cartel (Rodríguez Orejuela brothers). Major 2021 Paro Nacional protests with severe ESMAD violence. Now contested by FARC dissidents.

label: Marquetalia
sub: Site of May 1964 Colombian Army attack on Communist peasant enclave. Survivors founded FARC-EP. Considered the birthplace of the longest guerrilla insurgency in Western Hemisphere history.

label: Santa Marta
sub: First permanent Spanish city in Colombia (1525). Site of Simón Bolívar's death (December 17, 1830). Gateway to Tayrona National Park and Ciudad Perdida (Ciudad Tairona).

label: Ciudad Perdida (Teyuna)
sub: Tairona pre-Columbian city, ~650–800 CE, rediscovered 1972. Terraced urban complex of ~2,000 inhabitants. UNESCO World Heritage candidate. Symbol of pre-colonial civilization.

label: Lake Guatavita
sub: Sacred Muisca lake where El Dorado ritual took place — the new Zipa covered in gold dust cast offerings into the lake. Multiple Spanish attempts to drain it. Now a protected natural reserve.

label: Battle of Boyacá Bridge
sub: Site of decisive independence battle, 7 August 1819, where Bolívar's forces defeated Spanish royalists in under 2 hours. Colombia's most sacred national battlefield.

label: Cúcuta
sub: Site of Congress of Cúcuta (1821) that adopted Constitution of Gran Colombia. Major Venezuelan-Colombian border crossing; current humanitarian crisis hub with Venezuelan migration.

label: Ciénaga
sub: Site of Banana Massacre (December 1928) — army opened fire on United Fruit Company striking workers. Deaths disputed (47 officially; hundreds to 1,000+ by other accounts). Immortalized by García Márquez.

label: Putumayo
sub: Colombia's largest coca-producing department. Major target of Plan Colombia aerial fumigation. Currently a FARC dissident stronghold and route for cocaine to Ecuador and Peru.

label: Tumaco
sub: Pacific port and one of Colombia's most violent municipalities. Gateway for cocaine shipments to Mexico and Central America. High concentration of Afro-Colombian displacement victims.

label: San Basilio de Palenque
sub: First free African village in the Americas (c. 1714), founded by escaped enslaved people. UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage. Unique Palenquero language (Afro-Hispanic creole) still spoken.

label: La Catedral Prison
sub: Luxury hilltop prison Pablo Escobar designed himself for his 1991 'surrender'. He ran his cartel from here; escaped July 1992 when authorities tried to transfer him. Now demolished.

label: Havana Peace Talks (Bogotá reference)
sub: Colombia-FARC peace negotiations held in Havana, Cuba (2012–2016). Also current venue for Colombia-ELN peace talks (2022–present). Cuba and Norway served/serve as guarantors.

label: Buenaventura
sub: Colombia's most important Pacific port — handles ~60% of imports. Paradoxically one of Colombia's poorest and most violent cities; Afro-Colombian majority. Major cocaine export route.

label: Simacota (ELN Founding)
sub: Site of ELN's first public military operation, 7 January 1965 — attack on the Simacota police station in Santander. ELN manifesto read aloud afterwards. Marks the operational founding of the ELN.

label: San Agustín (Archaeological Park)
sub: UNESCO World Heritage site featuring the largest group of pre-Columbian funerary monuments and statues in South America, created by an unknown civilization 1st–8th century CE.

label: Arauca
sub: ELN's historical stronghold along the Venezuelan border. Key oil pipeline corridor (Caño Limón-Coveñas) repeatedly attacked by ELN for extortion. Currently contested between ELN and FARC dissidents.

label: Urabá / Clan del Golfo
sub: Gulf of Urabá region — headquarters of Clan del Golfo (also called AGC), Colombia's largest drug trafficking organization with ~8,000 fighters. Controls cocaine routes to Caribbean and Central America.

label: La Macarena
sub: Historical FARC heartland and secretariat zone in Meta department. Former demilitarized zone (Zona de Distensión) 1999–2002. Now controlled by FARC dissident Estado Mayor Central.

label: Bojayá Massacre Site
sub: 2 May 2002: FARC fired a homemade gas-cylinder bomb into the Bellavista church where ~300 civilians had sheltered, killing at least 119 people — mostly Afro-Colombian. Colombia's worst single-incident civilian massacre of the armed conflict.

label: Mapiripán Massacre
sub: 14–20 July 1997: AUC paramilitaries, with documented Colombian Army (7th Brigade) complicity, murdered at least 49 civilians over five days in this Meta municipality. The Inter-American Court found Colombia responsible in 2005.

label: El Salado Massacre
sub: 16–21 February 2000: AUC paramilitaries tortured and killed approximately 60 villagers in El Salado, Bolívar department — one of the deadliest AUC massacres. The Truth Commission documented systematic Army collusion that allowed paramilitaries unimpeded access.

label: Barrancabermeja
sub: Colombia's oil refinery capital (ECOPETROL) and historical ELN stronghold on the Magdalena River. ELN repeatedly bombed the Caño Limón-Coveñas pipeline here for extortion revenue. Late 1990s paramilitary 'cleansing' killed hundreds of union workers.

label: Palace of Justice (Siege 1985)
sub: 6–7 November 1985: M-19 guerrillas seized the Supreme Court building on Plaza de Bolívar. Military tank assault killed 98 people including 11 Supreme Court justices. Twelve cafeteria workers disappeared in state custody — the Inter-American Court found Colombia responsible.

label: Leticia (Amazon Triple Border)
sub: Colombia's Amazon capital at the tri-border with Brazil and Peru. Key cocaine transshipment corridor via Amazon river networks. Home to 30+ indigenous communities. Contested by FARC dissidents and Brazilian PCC criminal networks.

label: Trujillo Massacres
sub: 1988–1994: Series of massacres in Trujillo, Valle del Cauca perpetrated by drug trafficker Henry Loaiza ('El Alacrán') with documented police and army complicity. At least 342 victims, many dismembered with chainsaws. Colombia's first officially acknowledged state-linked massacre series.

label: Quibdó (Chocó)
sub: Capital of Chocó, Colombia's most Afro-Colombian and impoverished department, with the country's highest poverty rate (~70%). Among Colombia's highest forced displacement rates. Contested by FARC Estado Mayor Central, ELN, and Clan del Golfo for Pacific cocaine routes.
